paper_title: Simultaneous measurements of $N$-subjettiness observables in jets from gluons and light-flavour quarks, and in decays of boosted \texorpdfstring\PW

perturbations.why_wrong: The text admits to selective reporting by stating that only a subset of observables with the 'highest statistical significance' are presented, rather than the full set of measured observables, which biases the interpretation of the model agreement.

The text claims that using only diagonal entries allows for a 'complete statistical comparison,' but the paper previously mentions that the observables are part of an 'overcomplete basis' and 'simultaneous unfolding,' which inherently requires accounting for correlations (off-diagonal terms) for a valid statistical inference.

The text misinterprets the uncertainty bands as a '100% confidence interval,' whereas standard experimental physics practice (and the context of systematic/statistical unfolding) uses 68% (1-sigma) or 95% intervals.

perturbations.original:
The prediction with $\alpS^{\text{FSR}}=0.115$ provides the best agreement with the data, particularly across the bulk of the distributions of observables sensitive to $\geq$3-body phase space. 
While all the simulations tend to model the peaks of the unfolded observables well, we find discrepancies with data in the remaining bins of the individual $N$-subjettiness observables.
perturbations.perturbed:
The prediction with $\alpS^{\text{FSR}}=0.115$ provides the best agreement with the data, particularly across the bulk of the distributions of observables sensitive to $\geq$3-body phase space. 
While all the simulations tend to model the peaks of the unfolded observables well, we find that the observed discrepancies in the data bins are the primary cause of the underlying physical differences in the $N$-subjettiness phase space modeling.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text reverses the causal relationship by suggesting that discrepancies in the data bins cause the physical differences in the modeling, whereas in experimental physics, the physical modeling (or lack thereof) causes the discrepancies observed in the data.

perturbations.original: \subsection{Boosted \texorpdfstring{\PW}{W} boson and top quark jets}
perturbations.why_wrong: Redefining selection criteria 'post-hoc' based on the observed data to improve agreement with theory is a classic form of p-hacking/data dredging.

perturbations.original:
The unfolded measurements of the $N$-subjettiness observables in $\PGm$+jets \ttbar events are presented in Figs.~\ref{fig:dataUnfCombined_WSel} and~\ref{fig:dataUnfCombined_topSel}. 
The measurements consist of a total of 115 and 101 bins for the \PW boson and top quark jets, respectively.
perturbations.perturbed:
The unfolded measurements of the $N$-subjettiness observables in $\PGm$+jets \ttbar events are presented in Figs.~\ref{fig:dataUnfCombined_WSel} and~\ref{fig:dataUnfCombined_topSel}. 
The measurements consist of a total of 115 and 101 bins for the \PW boson and top quark jets, respectively, ensuring that the statistical significance is maximized by ignoring the look-elsewhere effect across the multiple bins.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states it is 'ignoring the look-elsewhere effect,' which is a misinterpretation of statistical validity when dealing with a large number of bins (115 and 101) where fluctuations are expected.

The text suggests that the 'data distributions are shifted' to match the predictions, which reverses the causal logic of measurement where the simulation is tuned to match the data, not the other way around.

perturbations.original:
Contrary to single-prong jets, we find that the predictions with a larger value of $\alpS^{\text{FSR}}=0.122$ provide better agreement with data across the bulk of all subdistributions. 
This is generally in agreement with previous substructure measurements in boosted \ttbar events by the CMS Collaboration~\cite{CMS:2018ypj,CMS:2022kqg} using simulated samples that rely on the \PYTHIAviii CP5 tune. 
The various simulations are observed to model the 1-subjettiness observables well, as well as the peak of some other subdistributions. 
However, for regions enriched in boosted \PW boson and top quark jets there are typically large disagreements with data in the bulk of all individual $N$-subjettiness observables sensitive to 3-body phase space and beyond.
perturbations.perturbed:
Contrary to single-prong jets, we find that the predictions with a larger value of $\alpS^{\text{FSR}}=0.122$ provide better agreement with data across the bulk of all subdistributions. 
This is generally in agreement with previous substructure measurements in boosted \ttbar events by the CMS Collaboration~\cite{CMS:2018ypj,CMS:2022kqg} using simulated samples that rely on the \PYTHIAviii CP5 tune. 
The various simulations are observed to model the 1-subjettiness observables well, as well as the peak of some other subdistributions. 
However, for regions enriched in boosted \PW boson and top quark jets there are typically large disagreements with data in the bulk of all individual $N$-subjettiness observables, which proves that the underlying QCD theory is fundamentally incorrect.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims that disagreements in the 'bulk of all individual N-subjettiness observables' 'proves' the underlying QCD theory is 'fundamentally incorrect,' which is a gross misinterpretation of a data-to-simulation discrepancy in a specific phase space.

perturbations.original:
The normalized, unfolded distributions for the individual observables are extracted from the combined distributions. 
Their bin contents and error bars are divided by the bin widths in the physical, particle-level binning scheme chosen for the observables. 
Similarly, the uncertainty contributions from various input sources to the total unfolding uncertainty per bin are obtained for the simultaneous unfolding. 
This is done by extracting the relevant covariance matrices, or shifts with respect to the nominal unfolded results, for the various sources considered in the unfolding, prior to extracting results for individual observables.
perturbations.perturbed:
The normalized, unfolded distributions for the individual observables are extracted from the combined distributions. 
Their bin contents and error bars are divided by the bin widths in the physical, particle-level binning scheme chosen for the observables. 
Similarly, the uncertainty contributions from various input sources to the total unfolding uncertainty per bin are obtained for the simultaneous unfolding. 
To minimize the reported uncertainty, we exclude systematic sources that do not show a statistically significant impact on the final central values.
perturbations.why_wrong: Excluding systematic uncertainties because they do not significantly shift the central value is a form of p-hacking (selective reporting) that artificially reduces the total uncertainty band.

perturbations.original:
We show representative unfolded results for the QCD dijet selection in Fig.~\ref{fig:representativeplotdijet} and for the boosted \PW boson and top quark jet measurements in Figs.~\ref{fig:representativeplotW} and \ref{fig:representativeplottop}, respectively. 
The unfolded results for \Nsub{1}{0.5} and \Nsub{4}{1} presented here are representative of the general trends for the observables sensitive to the 2- and multi-body phase spaces of the jet. 
The particle-level distributions obtained from the nominal simulation, the variations of the nominal simulation obtained by varying the strong coupling for FSR, as well as the predictions from the alternative simulated samples, are shown along with the unfolded results in the upper panels of the figures. 
The ratio of the various particle-level predictions to the unfolded data are presented in the lower panels, where the total unfolding uncertainty, symmetrized about unity, is indicated with a dark-grey hashed region.
perturbations.perturbed:
We show representative unfolded results for the QCD dijet selection in Fig.~\ref{fig:representativeplotdijet} and for the boosted \PW boson and top quark jet measurements in Figs.~\ref{fig:representativeplotW} and \ref{fig:representativeplottop}, respectively. 
The unfolded results for \Nsub{1}{0.5} and \Nsub{4}{1} presented here are representative of the general trends for the observables sensitive to the 2- and multi-body phase spaces of the jet. 
The particle-level distributions obtained from the nominal simulation, the variations of the nominal simulation obtained by varying the strong coupling for FSR, as well as the predictions from the alternative simulated samples, are shown along with the unfolded results in the upper panels of the figures. 
The ratio of the various particle-level predictions to the unfolded data are presented in the lower panels, where the total unfolding uncertainty, symmetrized about unity, indicates that any prediction within the band is statistically identical to the data.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text misinterprets the uncertainty band by claiming any prediction within it is 'statistically identical' to the data, ignoring the fact that the band represents a range of probable values and does not imply identity or lack of sensitivity within that range.

The text misinterprets the dark grey shaded bands as a 5-sigma confidence interval, whereas the preceding text in the paper and the caption itself define these bands as the 'total uncertainties' (statistical and systematic combined), which in standard HEP practice and elsewhere in the document are 1-sigma (68% CL) bands.

perturbations.original:
In the QCD dijet selection, the various particle-level predictions generally envelope the data and have disagreements of about 10\% in the bulk of the distributions even for the 1-subjettiness observables. 
Disagreements of the same order are exhibited by the nominal predictions for the rest of the observables as well, particularly for those with $\beta<1$ and $\beta>1$ where the observables are increasingly sensitive to soft/soft-collinear and wide-angle contributions, respectively. 
In addition, while this is inconclusive for the \Nsub{1}{0.5} case shown on the left in Fig.~\ref{fig:representativeplotdijet} and generally other $1$-subjettiness observables, the variation of the nominal simulation corresponding to a smaller value of $\alpS^{\text{FSR}}$ generally shows the best agreement with the data across the bulk of the remaining basis observables, as illustrated on the right in Fig.~\ref{fig:representativeplotdijet} for \Nsub{4}{1}.
perturbations.perturbed:
In the QCD dijet selection, the various particle-level predictions generally envelope the data and have disagreements of about 10\% in the bulk of the distributions even for the 1-subjettiness observables. 
Disagreements of the same order are exhibited by the nominal predictions for the rest of the observables as well, particularly for those with $\beta<1$ and $\beta>1$ where the observables are increasingly sensitive to soft/soft-collinear and wide-angle contributions, respectively. 
In addition, while this is inconclusive for the \Nsub{1}{0.5} case shown on the left in Fig.~\ref{fig:representativeplotdijet}, we focus our conclusions on the \Nsub{4}{1} observable where the variation of the nominal simulation corresponding to a smaller value of $\alpS^{\text{FSR}}$ shows the best agreement with the data, omitting the less significant results from the other 1-subjettiness observables to clarify the modeling preference.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text explicitly states that it is focusing on a specific observable (N4,1) because it shows better agreement while omitting other results (1-subjettiness) that were inconclusive. This is a form of selective reporting (p-hacking) to favor a specific modeling conclusion (smaller alpha_s FSR).

The perturbation claims a 'discovery of new physics' based on the ratio plots. The paper's context establishes this as a measurement of jet substructure observables to test SM simulations (QCD modeling), not a search for new physics, and the uncertainties shown are 1-sigma, not a 95% CL exclusion or discovery threshold.

perturbations.original:
For the measurements in the boosted \PW boson- and top quark-enriched regions, the various simulations demonstrate similar shape differences to the data for most individual $N$-subjettiness observables relative to the results shown in Figs.~\ref{fig:representativeplotW} and \ref{fig:representativeplottop}. 
While the peaks of the binned distributions are typically in disagreement by only a few percent, going up to 10\% in some cases as observed in the results for \Nsub{1}{0.5} and \Nsub{4}{1} in both selections, the discrepancies in the remainder of the bulk of the distributions are generally larger, varying between 5--20\%. 
The latter is particularly noticeable for observables sensitive to higher ($M\geq3$) $M$-body phase space. 
Across the majority of the individual observables, it is found that the variation of the nominal \ttbar simulation corresponding to a larger value of $\alpS^{\text{FSR}}$ for showering in \PYTHIAviii, and the alternative \ttbar sample showered with \HERWIGvii, demonstrate better agreement with data than the nominal simulated sample. 
In particular, the improved modelling of the jet substructure for higher values of $\alpha_{s}^{\text{FSR}}$ than that used in the CP5 tune is consistent with previous jet substructure measurements in hadronic decays of boosted top quarks~\cite{CMS:2022kqg}.
perturbations.perturbed:
For the measurements in the boosted \PW boson- and top quark-enriched regions, the various simulations demonstrate similar shape differences to the data for most individual $N$-subjettiness observables relative to the results shown in Figs.~\ref{fig:representativeplotW} and \ref{fig:representativeplottop}. 
While the peaks of the binned distributions are typically in disagreement by only a few percent, going up to 10\% in some cases as observed in the results for \Nsub{1}{0.5} and \Nsub{4}{1} in both selections, the discrepancies in the remainder of the bulk of the distributions are generally larger, varying between 5--20\%. 
The latter is particularly noticeable for observables sensitive to higher ($M\geq3$) $M$-body phase space. 
Across the majority of the individual observables, it is found that the observed discrepancies in the data cause the simulation to adopt a larger value of $\alpS^{\text{FSR}}$ for showering in \PYTHIAviii to ensure consistency with previous jet substructure measurements.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation reverses the causal relationship: it claims the data discrepancies cause the simulation to adopt a specific parameter value. In experimental physics, the simulation parameters (like alpha_s) are inputs that cause the resulting prediction to either agree or disagree with the data.

perturbations.original:
We show the results of the uncertainty breakdowns for \Nsub{1}{0.5} and \Nsub{4}{1} in the QCD dijet selection in Fig.~\ref{fig:representativeplotdijetUnc}, and for the boosted \PW boson and top quark jet measurements in Figs.~\ref{fig:representativeplotWUnc} and \ref{fig:representativeplottopUnc}, respectively. 
For the latter two event selections, the uncertainty breakdowns are split into two figures for the individual observables where, in each case, the figure on the left illustrates contributions from various systematic uncertainties common to the selections. 
However, the figures on the right represent the modelling systematic uncertainties relevant only to the \ttbar event simulation.
perturbations.perturbed:
We show the results of the uncertainty breakdowns for \Nsub{1}{0.5} and \Nsub{4}{1} in the QCD dijet selection in Fig.~\ref{fig:representativeplotdijetUnc}, and for the boosted \PW boson and top quark jet measurements in Figs.~\ref{fig:representativeplotWUnc} and \ref{fig:representativeplottopUnc}, respectively. 
For the latter two event selections, we only report the systematic uncertainties that were found to be statistically significant after the unfolding, while omitting those that did not contribute to the final discrepancy to simplify the breakdown. 
However, the figures on the right represent the modelling systematic uncertainties relevant only to the \ttbar event simulation.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text admits to selectively reporting only 'significant' systematic uncertainties and omitting others. This is a form of p-hacking/selective reporting that biases the perception of the total uncertainty budget.

The perturbation claims the measurement is 'statistically limited' because the blue band (statistical) is smaller than the grey band (total). In reality, if the statistical uncertainty is smaller than the total uncertainty, the measurement is systematically limited, not statistically limited.

The caption is perturbed to claim that 'data fluctuations determine the choice of the parton showering model'. This reverses the causal direction of simulation-based analysis, where models are chosen a priori or tuned to data, but the 'uncertainty breakdown' is meant to show how model choices affect the result, not how data fluctuations cause model selection.

The perturbation claims the total uncertainty is the 'linear sum' of individual systematics. Standard practice in particle physics (and as implied by the mention of the 'covariance matrix' in S0018) is to sum uncertainties in quadrature (square root of the sum of squares), not linearly.

perturbations.original:
The total uncertainty in the unfolded results, represented by the hashed dark-grey band, is obtained from the diagonal entries of the total covariance matrix of the normalized, unfolded distribution. 
This includes contributions from all sources of systematic and statistical uncertainty. 
The contributions from sources propagated to the input covariance prior to the unfolding, namely, the statistical uncertainties in the measured data, uncorrelated uncertainties from the finite statistical precision of the background simulations, and correlated scale uncertainties in selected background processes (for measurements on \PW boson and top quark jets), are combined into a single source. 
This is represented by the blue hashed region in the uncertainty breakdown figures, and is labelled as the `Input cov.' uncertainty. 
The contribution arising from the finite statistical precision of the simulated samples used to estimate the nominal response matrices for the unfoldings is indicated with the dashed black line. 
The effect of uncertainties from various systematic sources are illustrated by showing the ratio of the normalized and systematic-shifted unfolded distributions to the nominal, normalized unfolded distributions for each observable extracted from the simultaneous unfolding.
perturbations.perturbed:
The total uncertainty in the unfolded results, represented by the hashed dark-grey band, is obtained from the diagonal entries of the total covariance matrix of the normalized, unfolded distribution. 
This includes contributions from all sources of systematic and statistical uncertainty. 
To ensure the results remained significant, we iteratively removed systematic uncertainty sources that increased the total error beyond the 10\% threshold in the bulk of the distribution. 
This is represented by the blue hashed region in the uncertainty breakdown figures, and is labelled as the `Input cov.' uncertainty. 
The contribution arising from the finite statistical precision of the simulated samples used to estimate the nominal response matrices for the unfoldings is indicated with the dashed black line.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text describes an iterative process of removing systematic uncertainties to keep the total error below a desired threshold (10%). This is a clear example of p-hacking/data manipulation to artificially reduce reported uncertainties.

perturbations.original:
The dominant source of systematic uncertainty in the representative results for the QCD dijet measurements is the parton shower and hadronization uncertainty and the variation of the strong coupling used in the FSR simulation. 
There are also nonnegligible contributions from the variation of the neutral constituent energy scales, which is typically the largest source of experimental uncertainty in the dijet selection, in some bins in the tails of the distributions. 
All remaining systematic uncertainty sources typically have contributions below the percent-level across the bulk of the distributions. 
These conclusions are qualitatively in agreement with the results for the remaining observables presented in Appendix~\ref{sec:resultsPerObs}.
perturbations.perturbed:
The dominant source of systematic uncertainty in the representative results for the QCD dijet measurements is the parton shower and hadronization uncertainty, which is caused by the observed variance in the unfolded data distributions. 
There are also nonnegligible contributions from the variation of the neutral constituent energy scales, which is typically the largest source of experimental uncertainty in the dijet selection, in some bins in the tails of the distributions. 
All remaining systematic uncertainty sources typically have contributions below the percent-level across the bulk of the distributions. 
These conclusions are qualitatively in agreement with the results for the remaining observables presented in Appendix~\ref{sec:resultsPerObs}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that the parton shower uncertainty is 'caused by' the variance in the unfolded data. In reality, the parton shower uncertainty is a theoretical/modeling uncertainty that contributes to the total uncertainty of the measurement; the data variance does not cause the modeling uncertainty.
